title: Read the Community Site State
summary: Carry the same community-site files from untracked state into one staged proposal, then inspect what Git would record.

sketchBlocks.title: Check which files are untracked in the working tree
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

When a folder contains files but you haven't told Git about them yet, Git lists them as untracked. That tells you these files exist in your working tree but are not staged or recorded in any commit.

Example commands and their intent:

```bash
# show a human-readable summary with helpful commentary
git status

# show a compact machine-friendly list of paths
git status --short
```

In a worked example repository you might see output such as this from git status --short:

```text
?? site.md
?? home.html
?? contributing.md
?? team.md
?? logo.svg
```

Each line beginning with '??' is an untracked file. One row means a single path that Git is not yet tracking. Watching this list helps you avoid accidentally forgetting to include important files in your first snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: Move specific files into the staging area
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

Staging (with git add) takes the current contents of a file from the working tree and places that snapshot into the index (staging area). Staging prepares a specific set of paths for the next commit while leaving un-staged edits alone.

Example commands:

```bash
# stage two files for the next commit
git add CONTRIBUTING.md README.md

# check the short status to see staged vs unstaged
git status --short
```

After running these, git status --short might show a staged file with an 'A' (added) in the left column and unstaged changes with other markers. The staged snapshot represents exactly what Git will put into the next commit for those paths.

sketchBlocks.title: Read the staged vs unstaged columns with a compact status view
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

The short status view shows two columns of status characters. The left column reflects the index (staging area); the right column reflects the working tree. This compact view makes it easy to scan which files are staged for commit.

Example compact output (two-column annotations added for explanation):

```text
A  index.html     # left column 'A' means staged as added
?? about.md       # '??' means untracked (not staged)
 M styles.css     # right column 'M' means modified in working tree but not staged
```

Read the left column first to know what the next commit will contain, then read the right column to see remaining local edits you'll need to stage (or discard) separately.

sketchBlocks.title: View exactly what would be recorded in the next commit
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

Before committing, you can inspect the staged snapshot itself. git diff --staged shows the changes that are currently in the index compared with HEAD; git diff --staged --name-only lists only the staged path names. This is a safety check so you commit exactly what you expect.

Example commands:

```bash
# list only staged file paths
git diff --staged --name-only

# examine the staged content changes in unified diff form
git diff --staged
```

One row of output from git diff --staged --name-only corresponds to one staged path that will be part of the commit. Use this to confirm content before you run git commit.

quizDraft.title: Read the untracked community-site files
quizDraft.prompt: Your workspace contains a community-site repository root with five project files present but not yet staged. Use Git to list the repository state and confirm that the five files (README.md, index.html, about.md, contact.md, styles.css) are untracked. Type the command(s) and press Enter in the terminal to inspect the working tree.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Run the Git status command that shows a compact list of paths.

help.concept: Untracked files appear in the working tree but are not staged or committed; git status and git status --short reveal them.
help.hint_1: Use git status or git status --short to see untracked files listed with '??'.
help.hint_2: Run git status --short and check for lines beginning with '??' for each expected path.

quizDraft.title: Stage the five project files
quizDraft.prompt: Stage exactly these five project files so they become part of the index (staging area): README.md, index.html, about.md, contact.md, styles.css. Type the git add command(s) that stage those files and press Enter. After staging, show a short status to confirm the left column indicates staged adds.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use git add with the five explicit paths, then git status --short.

help.concept: git add records the working-tree contents of given paths into the index so they will be part of the next commit.
help.hint_1: Run git add README.md index.html about.md contact.md styles.css to stage those five files.
help.hint_2: After adding, run git status --short and look for an 'A' or other left-column marker for each staged path.

quizDraft.title: Interpret the short status view
quizDraft.prompt: A hidden setup has already staged the five project files for you. Run the compact status command that shows staged vs unstaged columns so you can read which files are staged in the left column. Type the command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Run the short status form of git status to see two columns of markers.

help.concept: git status --short shows a left column (index/staging area) and a right column (working tree) so you can quickly see what will be committed.
help.hint_1: The command is git status --short (or git status -s).
help.hint_2: Look at the left column characters to confirm those five paths are staged (e.g., 'A' for added).

quizDraft.title: View the staged snapshot contents
quizDraft.prompt: Show what changes are currently staged (the snapshot in the index) so you can confirm the content that would be recorded by the next commit. Type the git diff command that inspects the staged snapshot and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use git diff --staged or git diff --staged --name-only to inspect staged changes or path names.

help.concept: git diff --staged compares the index to HEAD to show exactly what content will be recorded in the next commit.
help.hint_1: Run git diff --staged --name-only to list only staged paths, or git diff --staged to see content diffs.
help.hint_2: Use the staged snapshot output to verify there are exactly the five intended paths and no unexpected changes before committing.

quizDraft.title: Tracked vs untracked files
quizDraft.prompt: Which statement best describes an untracked file in a Git repository?
options: The file is recorded in the repository history and safely versioned.
The file exists in the working directory but is not staged or committed by Git.
The file is staged and will be part of the next commit.
The file is ignored by .gitignore and will never be shown by git status.
quizDraft.hint: Untracked files exist in the working directory but haven't been recorded in the index or history.

help.concept: An untracked file is present in the working tree but Git has no staged snapshot or record of it; it will appear with '??' in git status --short.
help.hint_1: An untracked file is not yet in the index and will not be included in commits until staged with git add.
help.hint_2: Look for the choice that says the file is present locally but not recorded by Git yet.

quizDraft.title: Why inspect staged snapshot
quizDraft.prompt: Why should you run git diff --staged before creating a first commit?
options: It shows the full commit history for the repository.
It confirms the exact staged content that will be part of the next commit.
It removes untracked files from the working tree.
quizDraft.hint: This command compares the index to HEAD so you can confirm the exact content that will be recorded.

help.concept: git diff --staged shows the actual content differences staged for the next commit so you avoid committing unexpected changes.
help.hint_1: It lists the content changes that are currently in the index compared with HEAD.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that mentions confirming exactly what will be recorded by the next commit.

quizDraft.title: Which commands place changes into the index (stage changes)?
quizDraft.prompt: Select all commands that can create or modify the staging-area snapshot (the index).
quizDraft.hint: Think about commands that record a change into the index rather than only creating commits.

help.concept: Several Git commands update the index: git add stages new/updated paths; git mv stages the rename (it is equivalent to a move + staging); git rm stages removals.
help.hint_1: Commands that alter the index include git add and operations that imply staging such as git mv and git rm.
help.hint_2: git commit records what's already in the index; it does not itself stage new file edits.

quizDraft.title: Order the local Git snapshot workflow steps
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange these steps in the order they happen locally when preparing and recording the first snapshot for a folder.
tokens: Make an edit in the working tree
Record the snapshot with git commit
Verify with git status that the working tree is clean
correctOrder: Make an edit in the working tree
Record the snapshot with git commit
Verify with git status that the working tree is clean
quizDraft.hint: Think from making an edit to verifying a clean working tree after a commit.

help.concept: A local snapshot workflow goes from editing (working tree) → staging (index) → committing (record) → verifying the clean state.
help.hint_1: Start with making an edit in the working tree, then stage, then commit, then verify with git status.
help.hint_2: Choose the sequence that begins with an edit and ends with a verification step like git status or a clean working tree.

quizDraft.title: Command to show compact status
quizDraft.prompt: [blank1] --short shows a compact two-column Git status indicating staged and unstaged changes.
quizDraft.template: [blank1] --short shows a compact two-column Git status indicating staged and unstaged changes.
quizDraft.hint: Which git command accepts --short to give a compact status?

help.concept: the missing term --short (or -s) prints a compact two-column status view showing index vs working-tree changes.
help.hint_1: The command that shows status is the missing term.
help.hint_2: Pick the git command name (not a subcommand like commit or add).

quizDraft.title: Place a file into the index
quizDraft.prompt: To prepare README.md for inclusion in the next commit, run git add [blank1].
quizDraft.template: To prepare README.md for inclusion in the next commit, run git add [blank1].
quizDraft.hint: The blank should be the path you want to stage.

help.concept: git add <path> records the working-tree contents of the path into the index for the next commit.
help.hint_1: Fill the blank with a file path present in the working tree.
help.hint_2: Use the literal filename the missing term as the path to stage that specific file.
